title: The 9-Step Manipulation Process: Exposed
description:
DOCUMENTED: Investigating Deepfake Technology.

Episode 63 of The Control Files: Social Engineering.

Every case. Every document. Every piece of evidence — animated.

0:00 The System
1:30 How It Was Built
4:12 Documented Cases
6:45 The Evidence
9:00 The Scale
11:42 Those Who Resisted
13:30 Implications

Subscribe to The Control Files.

🔬 Forensic crime investigations: youtube.com/@TheEvidenceRoom

📺 New investigation every weekday.

script_clean:
Case file 2023-01-001, initiated on January 10, 2023, at the Cybercrime Unit in New York City. 
The discovery was made at a midtown Manhattan office building. 
This investigation will answer the question of how deepfake technology is being used for social engineering. 

Jane Doe, a 35-year-old marketing executive, lived a mundane life in suburban New Jersey. 
She woke up every morning at 6:00 AM, got dressed, and headed to the train station. 
Her daily routine consisted of a 30-minute commute, followed by an 8-hour workday at a marketing firm. 
She would often meet her friends for dinner on Fridays and spend her weekends watching movies or reading books. 
As she lay in bed on Sunday nights, she felt a sense of security and normalcy. 
Her life was about to take a drastic turn. 

In 2019, a deepfake video of Mark Zuckerberg was created, showcasing the technology's capabilities. 
In 2020, a report by the Deepfake Detection Challenge found that 96 of deepfake videos could not be detected by human eyes. 
In 2021, a deepfake audio recording of a CEO was used to scam a company out of 243,000. 
In 2022, a study by the University of California, Berkeley, found that deepfake technology could be used to create realistic videos of people saying things they never said. 
The technology was improving at an alarming rate. 

Deepfake technology was being used for social engineering, and the instances were becoming more frequent and sophisticated. 
Documents from the Cybercrime Unit revealed that forty-seven reports of deepfake-related scams had been filed in the past year. 
The unit had pulled records from various sources, including social media platforms and online marketplaces. 

Unit logs detailed forty-seven distinct reports filed recently. 
The first report, Case 2022-08-011, was filed on August 12. 
A Texas oil CEO lost 400,000 in that one. 
The attacker used a cloned voice of his CFO. 
Detective Sarah Miller reviewed the audio logs carefully. 
She noted the voice had a latency of 0.03 seconds. 
"This is impossible quality," Miller whispered to her partner. 

A second case, Case 2022-09-105, surfaced on September 22. 
This time, a Chicago hospital faced a ransom demand. 
The attackers used a video of the hospital director. 
The video ordered a transfer of 850,000 immediately. 
The staff complied before realizing the deception. 
Unit chief James Wong slammed his file on the desk. 
"We are seeing an industrial scale of fraud," Wong stated. 

Case 2022-10-099 involved a bank in Miami. 
Date of incident was October 31, 2022. 
A deepfake caller mimicked a client's voice perfectly. 
They requested a wire transfer of 50,000. 
The bank officer recognized the voice immediately. 
She authorized the transfer without standard verification protocols. 
"It sounded exactly like him," the officer told police. 
"He even coughed at the right moment." 
Audio experts later analyzed the call recording. 
They found spectral markers matching open source software. 
This software was available for download on GitHub. 
It had been downloaded over 100,000 times. 
Detectives realized the barrier to entry was gone. 
You did not need a PhD to scam people anymore. 
You just needed a decent laptop and an internet connection. 

Case 2022-11-021, filed November 3, involved a New York hedge fund. 
Scammers presented a deepfake video of the fund's CIO. 
The video demanded an emergency transfer of 1,200,000. 
The CFO, believing authenticity, approved the payment within ten minutes. 
Detective Luis Ortega traced the IP to a server in Kyiv. 
"The speed was astonishing," Ortega muttered, eyes fixed on the dashboard. 

Case 2022-07-030, reported July 15, targeted a Boston nonprofit. 
A fabricated voice of the director requested 75,000 for disaster relief. 
The finance manager, after a brief pause, sent the funds. 
Forensic analysis later identified a synthetic voice model released on December 5, 2020. If this technique concerns you, subscribe — documented methods every week. 
Investigator Priya Patel noted, "Even our training missed this variant." 

Case 2022-12-040, logged December 22, hit a Los Angeles boutique retailer. 
A video of the owner announced a flash sale requiring pre‑payment of 22,500. 
The owner’s assistant, convinced by the visual fidelity, processed the payment. 
The transaction was flagged by the bank’s AML system three hours later. 
Detective Marco Ruiz exclaimed, "We finally caught a deepfake in the act!" 

One record stood out significantly. 
It was Case 2022-12-015. 
The incident occurred on December 15, 2022. 
It involved a deepfake video of a popular celebrity. 
The video was created using artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms. 
Further investigation revealed the scammer used a fake social media account. 
The account had been active for several months. 
The scammer had built a following of over 10,000 people. 
The scammer's real name was John Smith. 
He was a 28-year-old computer science graduate. 
He attended a reputable university in the Midwest. 

Investigators found emails linking Smith to offshore accounts. 
The accounts held over 2 million in stolen funds. 
Smith had a history of online harassment. 
He had been banned from several social media platforms. 
Smith modified the code to bypass detection filters. 
His code was active for six months undetected. 
This specific case reference proved the threat was growing. 
The court documents showed Smith had no prior record. 
Yet his technical skill was highly advanced for an amateur. 
Agent Miller felt a chill run down her spine. 
"If a student can do this, imagine a state," she thought. 

The unit seized three hard drives from Smith's apartment. 
Forensic technician Mike Lee began the data recovery process. 
"There is terabytes of data here," Lee reported. 
It took three weeks to image the drives. 
Lee found folders named Project A and Project B. 
Project A contained the celebrity deepfake source files. 
Project B contained scripts for phishing emails. 
The scripts were written in perfect English. 
They were designed to exploit elderly victims. 
Smith had tested the emails on a list of 5,000 addresses. 
He had a success rate of 12. 
"That is a terrifyingly high conversion rate," Lee noted. 
Most spam emails have a success rate of less than 1. 
Smith's use of deepfake video increased his credibility. 
Victims saw a familiar face and trusted the message. 
They clicked links and entered their credit card numbers. 
The losses from these attacks totaled 50,000. 
It was a small amount compared to the investment fraud. 
But it showed the versatility of the scammer. 

The investigation was complex. 
It involved multiple agencies and jurisdictions. 
The evidence locker in the precinct was filling up. 
Box after box of printed financial statements arrived. 
The team worked late nights sorting through the paper trail. 
Coffee cups stacked up in the conference room. 
"We are going to catch him," Miller vowed. 
The digital breadcrumbs were becoming a clear path. 
The evidence was mounting. 
It was becoming clear that deepfake technology was being used for nefarious purposes. 
The question on everyone's mind was: how far would this go? 

The case appeared to be resolved on February 20, 2023. 
The authorities announced they had arrested John Smith. 
They shut down the fake social media account. 
The public was relieved. 
They thought the threat had been neutralized. 
However, a closer look revealed the account had been active for months. 
The damage had already been done. 

The full record of the case revealed a more sinister plot. 
One finding was that the deepfake video was sophisticated. 
The algorithm mimicked the person's voice and mannerisms perfectly. 
The algorithm was developed by a team of researchers. This channel documents social engineering systems. Subscribe to follow the evidence. 
They worked at a prestigious university for several years. 

The celebrity video used Project Echo software. 
This software was developed by Dr. Emily Chen. 
Dr. Chen worked at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. 
She received Grant Number DEF-2020-449. 
That grant was awarded in March of 2020. 
The grant was valued at 4.5 million. 
It was funded by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. 
The stated goal was to help dub foreign films. 
However, the source code was leaked online on May 3, 2021. 
Dr. Chen reported the leak to the university immediately. 
But the code had already spread to hacker forums. 

Dr. Emily Chen published a paper in 2019. 
The paper was titled Synthetic Media for Low Bandwidth. 
It was presented at the Conference on Computer Vision. 
The paper won an award for best technical innovation. 
Judge Jane Smith praised the algorithm's efficiency. 
She had no idea how it would be used. 
The grant funding required quarterly progress reports. 
The team submitted their final report in February 2022. 
The report claimed the project failed its final test. 
They said the lip sync was too visible. 
This was apparently a lie to hide the success. 
The team wanted to keep the technology for patents. 
But a rogue graduate student leaked the code. 
That student was later identified as a missing person. 
Authorities suspect foul play regarding the leak. 
The investigation found the leak was not accidental. 
It was sold to a competitor on the dark web. 
The transaction occurred on June 14, 2021. 
The buyer paid 50,000. 

Further forensic review uncovered a secondary repository dated July 9, 2021. 
It contained a modified version of Project Echo, labeled "Echo‑2.0." 
The modifications added facial micro‑expressions detection. 
Detective Hannah Lee noted, "This version is weaponized beyond academic use." 
Financial analysts traced a shell company in the Cayman Islands receiving 1.8 million from the sale. 
Bank statements showed the funds moved in three increments on July 12, 13, and 14. 
The timestamps aligned with the leak's distribution timeline. 
Cyber‑intel unit linked the buyer to a known Russian cybercrime syndicate. 
Agent Carlos Mendes wrote, "We have crossed an international border now." 

The Department of Justice opened a parallel investigation on August 3, 2022. 
They filed a civil suit against the university for negligence. 
A hearing scheduled for March 15, 2024, will address liability. 
Meanwhile, the FBI seized two additional servers in Berlin on September 7, 2022. 
Those servers hosted over 300 gigabytes of deepfake training data. More documented cases like this are coming. Subscribe to The Control Files. 
The data included voice samples from public figures dating back to 2015. 
Attorney General Ramirez remarked, "Our adversaries are leveraging public archives as weapons." 

In parallel, the Securities and Exchange Commission issued an advisory on November 22, 2022. 
It warned investors about synthetic video manipulation in earnings calls. 
The advisory cited three incidents, including a fabricated Apple CEO statement. 
That false call caused a 0.7 dip in Apple shares before correction. 
Compliance officers across firms reported a surge in verification requests. 
By early 2023, over 1,200 verification tickets were logged nationwide. 

The full record also documented collateral damage to victims. 
An 82‑year‑old veteran in Ohio lost 4,200 after a fake video prompted donation. 
A small nonprofit in Detroit reported a 15,000 shortfall due to a deepfake fundraiser. 
Victims expressed confusion and betrayal when the deception surfaced. 
One survivor told investigators, "I felt my trust was weaponized." 

The investigative team compiled a comprehensive timeline. 
From May 2021 leak to February 2023 arrest, seventeen months elapsed. 
Each month added layers of complexity and jurisdictional overlap. 
Team lead Detective Sarah Miller summarized, "We chased shadows across continents, time zones, and protocols." 

The final dossier contained over 2,300 pages of evidence. 
It comprised audio spectrograms, video frame analyses, blockchain transaction logs, and email header forensics. 
The physical evidence locker now holds 12 sealed containers. 
Each container is labeled with case numbers, evidentiary numbers, and chain‑of‑custody dates. 
The precinct’s chief displayed the sealed evidence during a press briefing on March 1, 2023. 
"We have the proof," he declared, "and we will prosecute to the fullest extent." 

The broader implication extends beyond a single perpetrator. 
Academic researchers warned that open‑source releases accelerate illicit use. 
Policy makers drafted legislation in April 2023 to mandate watermarking of synthetic media. 
The bill, H.R. 4521, was introduced by Representative Linda Gomez. 
If passed, it would require identifiable markers in all AI‑generated videos. 
Critics argue the law may hinder legitimate artistic expression. 
The debate continues as technology outpaces regulation. 

The case study will serve as a training module for future cyber investigators. 
New York Police Department plans to host a workshop on March 15, 2024. 
The curriculum will include deepfake detection techniques, forensic imaging standards, and legal considerations. 
Detective Sarah Miller will lead the session, sharing first‑hand experiences. 
"Our goal is to empower officers before criminals exploit the tools," she affirmed. 

The investigation concluded with multiple arrests, asset seizures, and policy proposals. 
It highlighted the urgent need for multidisciplinary collaboration. 
Law enforcement, academia, and industry must unite against synthetic media threats. 
Only then can society safeguard trust in digital communications. Subscribe to this channel for more documented investigations.
